title: Practice Exam #6 (Cloud Database Engineer)
description: Google Cloud Certified Professional Cloud Database Engineer - 50 questions

questions.text: A financial services company operates a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in europe-west1 as its primary transactional database. A cross-region read replica has been configured in europe-west3 as part of the company's disaster recovery strategy. During an upcoming compliance audit, the database engineering team must demonstrate that the organization can shift production database operations to the secondary region if a catastrophic failure renders the primary region completely inaccessible. The demonstration must show that the secondary region can serve read-write traffic with minimal data loss. How should the team validate this regional disaster recovery capability?

options.text: Promote the cross-region read replica to become an independent standalone primary instance
options.explanation:
Promoting the cross-region read replica is the correct approach to validating regional disaster recovery. When a cross-region replica is promoted, Cloud SQL severs the replication link and elevates the replica to a fully independent primary instance capable of handling both read and write operations. This process directly tests whether the secondary region can assume the primary role, and it allows the team to verify application connectivity, DNS updates, connection string changes, and data consistency in the target region. This is the most realistic and effective way to prove that the organization can recover from a total loss of the primary region.
Failing over to a high availability standby only moves the workload to a different zone within the same region, which does not help when the entire region is unavailable. Restoring from an automated backup into a new instance in the secondary region is a legitimate recovery technique, but it takes considerably longer than promoting a live replica and does not validate the replication infrastructure that is already in place. Deleting the primary instance is a destructive and reckless action that would trigger an internal zonal failover rather than a cross-region transition, and it would not prove the secondary region is prepared to handle production traffic during a genuine regional disaster.

questions.text: Your company is migrating a legacy Oracle-based inventory management system to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL using Database Migration Service. The continuous migration job has been running for several hours, and most tables are replicating correctly. However, your team discovers that a heavily used order tracking table shows a successful schema transfer but zero rows replicated to the destination. Network connectivity has been validated, the source database is accessible, and the migration service account has all required privileges on the source. What must be done to resolve the data replication issue with this particular table?

options.text: Define a primary key on the table
options.explanation:
The most likely cause of this issue is that the order tracking table lacks a primary key. Database Migration Service uses logical replication to capture and apply changes from the source to the destination. Logical replication requires a mechanism to uniquely identify each row so that inserts, updates, and deletes can be accurately tracked and replicated. When a table does not have a primary key, the migration service cannot reliably determine which specific row has changed, and it will skip that table during the data synchronization phase. This is why the schema can be created successfully while no actual data flows through. Adding a primary key gives the replication process the unique row identifier it needs to function properly and is a common prerequisite across most change data capture systems.
Enabling supplemental logging is a configuration step on the Oracle source that ensures additional column data is written to redo logs, but it does not address the fundamental problem of missing unique row identification. Removing NCLOB columns might resolve certain data type compatibility challenges or reduce overhead during migration, but it would not explain why an entire table is excluded from replication. Converting from index-organized to heap-organized storage changes the physical layout of data within Oracle but has no bearing on whether logical replication can identify and track individual rows. The scenario specifically describes a table where the schema was created but no data is flowing, which is the classic symptom of a missing primary key in a logical replication setup.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm maintains patient appointment records in a Firestore database. Compliance requirements mandate that a full database export be performed every night at midnight and stored in a Cloud Storage bucket. The engineering team wants to minimize operational overhead and avoid paying for idle resources. What is the most appropriate way to automate this recurring export?

options.text: Set up a Cloud Run service to execute a containerized script that handles the export operations
options.explanation:
The best approach here is combining Cloud Scheduler with a Cloud Function to automate the nightly Firestore export. Cloud Scheduler serves as a fully managed cron service that can trigger a lightweight Cloud Function on a defined schedule. The Cloud Function then calls the Firestore export API to write the data to Cloud Storage. Because both services are serverless, there is no infrastructure to maintain, and the team only pays for the brief execution time of the function each night. Built-in retry mechanisms also reduce the risk of missed backups without requiring manual monitoring.
Using a Compute Engine VM with a local crontab means the team must manage an operating system, apply patches, and pay for the instance even during the many hours it sits idle between exports. App Engine does support cron-based scheduling, but it introduces more complexity than necessary when the task is simply invoking an API call once per day. Cloud Run is a capable platform, but it requires containerizing the export script, which adds unnecessary packaging and deployment steps for such a straightforward job. Any approach that relies on persistent infrastructure increases both cost and the chance of failure if that infrastructure becomes unavailable.

questions.text: A global logistics company runs a transaction-intensive application on a multi-region Cloud Spanner deployment. The application processes a very high volume of data mutations with almost no read queries. The operations team reports that transaction commit latency has been increasing and wants to reduce it without changing the database schema or deployment topology. What should you do?

options.text: Add more read-only replicas across additional regions
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to turn off leader-aware routing in the Cloud Spanner client library. In a multi-region configuration, the client library by default tries to discover and route requests directly to the leader replica. While this can help with certain read patterns, it introduces overhead in write-heavy scenarios because the client spends time and resources tracking leader transitions and maintaining metadata about which replica currently holds the leader role. Disabling this feature lets the client send requests to the nearest available replica and allows the Spanner backend to handle internal routing more efficiently. This is a Google-recommended optimization specifically suited to workloads dominated by writes with minimal read activity, and it directly addresses the commit latency concern without requiring schema or topology changes.
Enabling leader-aware routing would move in the wrong direction because the feature is already contributing to the overhead that causes increased latency in a write-dominated workload. The additional client-side processing to locate and track the leader adds unnecessary delay when nearly all operations are mutations. Adding read-only replicas would expand read capacity across regions, but since the application performs almost no reads, this change would have no meaningful impact on write commit latency. Building indexes on frequently written columns would actually make the problem worse because each index must be updated with every write operation, increasing the amount of work Spanner must do during each transaction commit and adding synchronization overhead across regions.

questions.text: A global logistics company operates a 6 TB Cloud SQL for MySQL instance that powers real-time shipment tracking and inventory management across multiple regions. Regulatory audits require a complete weekly data extract delivered to Cloud Storage. The engineering team has been instructed that the export process must not degrade query performance during peak hours and should not require manual provisioning or teardown of additional database infrastructure. Which approach best satisfies these requirements?

options.text: Deploy a read replica and perform a sequential table export
options.explanation:
The Cloud SQL serverless export capability is the ideal solution here because it delegates the entire export workload to a temporary, hidden resource that Google manages behind the scenes. The primary instance does not have to dedicate its own compute or memory to processing the large data transfer, which means shipment tracking queries and inventory lookups continue running without performance degradation. Because Google automatically handles the provisioning, scaling, and cleanup of the temporary export environment, the engineering team avoids any manual infrastructure management. This makes it the lowest-overhead option that still delivers a full weekly extract to Cloud Storage as required by the audit policy.
Running mysqldump directly on the primary instance would consume CPU and memory that live traffic depends on, which directly conflicts with the requirement to avoid query performance degradation. Creating a temporary clone of the instance would technically isolate the export workload, but it forces the team to manually provision and later tear down the clone each week, adding operational complexity and cost that the instructions specifically prohibit. Setting up a read replica and running a sequential table export introduces a persistent piece of infrastructure that must be maintained, and a full 6 TB export from a replica can cause replication lag that results in inconsistent data in the extract. Both the clone and replica approaches shift the burden onto the engineering team to orchestrate timing, monitor progress, and manage lifecycle, none of which aligns with the stated goal of a hands-off, non-disruptive export process.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is building a data pipeline that ingests patient records into multiple microservices hosted across several service projects within a Shared VPC. A database engineer needs to provision a new Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in one of the service projects and ensure it is reachable only through internal IP addresses from the host project's VPC network. During the provisioning attempt, the engineer encounters a "Network association failed" error when trying to configure private IP connectivity. The instance cannot be created. What should the engineer do to resolve this issue?

options.text: Enable the Service Networking API in the host project and retry the instance creation
options.explanation:
The resolution here is to set up Private Services Access on the VPC network prior to attempting the Cloud SQL deployment. Private Services Access involves two key steps: allocating a reserved IP address range within your VPC and then creating a private connection that peers your VPC network with the Google-managed services network where Cloud SQL instances actually run. Without completing both of these steps, the Cloud SQL control plane has no mechanism to assign an internal IP address that is routable within your network. The "Network association failed" error occurs precisely because there is no established peering relationship or reserved address space that allows the managed database to bind to your VPC. In a Shared VPC setup, this configuration is done on the host project's VPC network, which is the network shared across the service projects.
Enabling the Service Networking API is a prerequisite that must be active, but simply enabling an API does not create the actual network peering or allocate the IP range needed to bridge the VPC and the Google-managed network. Manually creating a VPC Network Peering connection from the Google-managed project is not possible because you do not have access to or administrative control over the project that Google uses to host Cloud SQL infrastructure. Switching to a public IP with authorized networks would technically bypass the network association requirement, but it directly violates the stated constraint that the instance must only be reachable through internal IP addresses. The underlying problem is the absence of a configured Private Services Access connection, and none of the other options address that root cause.

questions.text: A healthcare startup is building a mobile patient check-in application that will be deployed across 300+ clinics nationwide. Patients use tablets at each clinic to check in, fill out intake forms, and confirm their insurance details. The application must remain fully functional even when a clinic experiences a network outage, and all data must automatically sync to the cloud once connectivity returns. The startup has a small engineering team and wants to avoid building custom synchronization logic or managing database infrastructure at each clinic location. Which database architecture should you recommend?

options.text: Build a cross-platform native application with a local embedded database at each clinic and use Cloud Spanner as the centralized backend database
options.explanation:
Firebase with Cloud Firestore is the right choice because Firestore's client SDKs provide built-in offline persistence that automatically caches data on the local device and synchronizes changes back to the cloud when network connectivity is restored. This means the check-in tablets at each clinic can continue accepting patient data during outages without any custom sync logic. Firebase as a platform also eliminates the need to provision or manage database instances at individual clinic locations, which directly addresses the startup's goal of minimizing infrastructure management with a small team. Scaling across 300+ clinics happens naturally since there are no local database servers to maintain.
The option involving SQLite with Cloud SQL would require the engineering team to design and implement their own offline caching and data synchronization layer between the local SQLite database and Cloud SQL, adding significant development and maintenance burden. Similarly, the option pairing Room databases with AlloyDB presents the same challenge since neither Room nor AlloyDB provides an automatic cloud sync mechanism out of the box. Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed relational database that would be excessive for a clinic check-in application, and it still does not solve the offline data problem at the tablet level without a custom-built local data layer and sync framework. In all three of these alternatives, the team would essentially be recreating the offline-first capabilities that Firestore already delivers natively through its SDK.

questions.text: A multinational gaming company stores player profile and matchmaking data in Cloud Bigtable. Players connect from data centers in the US, Europe, and Southeast Asia. The engineering team wants to guarantee that each regional deployment reads and writes to the nearest cluster to achieve the lowest latency possible, while also keeping cross-region network transfer costs to a minimum. They have a requirement to use a single Bigtable instance to take advantage of automatic replication across regions. Which configuration best meets these requirements?

options.text: One instance with three clusters in different regions and a single app profile using multi-cluster routing
options.explanation:
The best approach is to create one Bigtable instance with clusters deployed in each of the three regions and then configure three app profiles, each using single-cluster routing pointed at the local cluster. This ensures that each regional application deployment sends traffic only to the cluster in its own region, which delivers the lowest latency and avoids expensive cross-region data transfer. Because all three clusters live within the same instance, Bigtable automatically replicates data between them, keeping player profiles consistent across regions without any additional infrastructure.
Using a single app profile with multi-cluster routing would allow Bigtable to route requests to any of the three clusters, which means a temporary issue in one region could cause traffic to be redirected across the globe, increasing both latency and network costs unexpectedly. Creating three separate instances would break automatic replication, since Bigtable only replicates data between clusters within the same instance. Without replication, the team would need to build and maintain a custom synchronization mechanism, which contradicts the stated goal of leveraging built-in replication. The option combining three separate instances with three app profiles per instance also fails to meet the single-instance requirement and would similarly lack cross-region replication. Single-cluster routing with dedicated app profiles is the only way to pin traffic to a specific regional cluster while still benefiting from the replication that a multi-cluster instance provides.

questions.text: A financial services company operates a Cloud Spanner database that handles high-frequency trading transactions. The database was recently converted from a single-region deployment to a multi-regional configuration to meet new disaster recovery requirements. After the migration, the team observes that read latency has stayed within acceptable limits, but trade execution writes are experiencing noticeably higher latency compared to the previous setup. You want to address the increased write latency without reverting to a single-region deployment. What should you do?

options.text: Add additional read-write replicas to the Spanner instance
options.explanation:
The best approach is to ensure the application components responsible for write operations are located geographically close to the leader region. In a multi-regional Cloud Spanner configuration, all write transactions are coordinated by the leader replica using the Paxos consensus protocol. When write requests originate from a region far from the leader, the round-trip network distance adds substantial latency to every transaction. By placing the services that generate writes in or near the default leader region, you dramatically reduce this physical distance and the associated delay. This is the most direct solution to the latency increase that commonly occurs when transitioning from a regional to a multi-regional Spanner topology.
Enabling data boost provisions separate compute resources intended to support analytical and batch workloads, and it does not accelerate standard transactional write operations. Configuring bounded staleness reads allows replicas to serve slightly outdated data to improve read performance, but this setting has no effect whatsoever on write latency since writes must still be coordinated through the leader. Increasing the number of read-write replicas might seem beneficial, but more replicas actually means more nodes need to participate in consensus during a write, which can further increase rather than decrease write latency. None of these alternatives address the fundamental issue, which is the network distance between the application issuing writes and the leader replica that must coordinate them.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company uses AlloyDB for PostgreSQL to manage patient records and clinical data. The primary instance processes continuous write-heavy operations from electronic health record (EHR) integrations throughout the day. A research team periodically runs intensive aggregation queries to generate population health reports, which degrades the performance of the EHR ingestion pipeline. Management wants to isolate the reporting workload from the transactional system and minimize infrastructure spending during overnight hours when the research team is inactive. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Deploy a primary instance alongside a large fixed-size read pool instance dedicated to all reporting queries
options.explanation:
The correct approach here is to pair the primary instance with a read pool instance that leverages AlloyDB autoscaling based on a 75% CPU utilization target. AlloyDB read pool instances support autoscaling, which dynamically adds or removes nodes depending on CPU load. By targeting 75% utilization, the system maintains enough headroom to absorb traffic spikes while avoiding over-provisioning. When the research team runs demanding aggregation queries during the day, the read pool automatically expands to handle the load. At night, when no reporting is happening, the pool contracts and costs drop accordingly. This keeps the primary instance fully dedicated to the write-heavy EHR pipeline without interference.
Running both workloads on a single primary instance invites resource contention that will directly impact the latency and throughput of the EHR ingestion process. Deploying a large fixed-size read pool instance eliminates the contention problem but wastes money during overnight hours when the research team is not active and the extra capacity sits idle. Manually scaling the read pool up in the morning and down in the evening might seem practical, but it cannot respond to unexpected query volume changes during the day and requires ongoing human intervention to maintain. Only the CPU-based autoscaling policy adapts in real time to actual demand without requiring anyone to monitor or adjust the infrastructure.

questions.text: Your team operates a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance that powers a monthly financial summary dashboard used by department managers. The database is refreshed with new data at the beginning of each month via an ETL pipeline from Cloud Storage. Between refreshes, the database handles only read queries from a small number of internal users. Company policy requires all data to remain within a designated region due to data sovereignty rules. You want to keep costs as low as possible while still being able to recover the database within a day if something goes wrong. What configuration should you use?

options.text: Enable high availability and rely on the default multi-regional backup storage location
options.explanation:
The best approach is to disable high availability and use automated backups stored in a regional bucket. This workload is low-frequency and non-critical, with data refreshed only once a month and consumed by a small group of users running read-only queries. A 24-hour recovery window is generous enough that restoring from a backup is perfectly adequate, making the additional expense of a standby instance unnecessary. High availability roughly doubles compute costs because it maintains an identical standby instance in another zone, which provides no meaningful benefit here. Storing the automated backups in a regional location satisfies the data sovereignty requirement by keeping all data within the designated region.
Enabling high availability would introduce unnecessary cost by provisioning a standby instance that sits idle, which makes no sense when the recovery objective is measured in hours rather than minutes. Using the default multi-regional backup location would also risk placing backup data outside the required region, potentially violating data sovereignty rules. Creating a cross-region replica directly violates the policy that all data must remain within a single designated region, regardless of any disaster recovery benefits it might offer. Deploying a read replica in the same region adds compute and storage costs to handle read traffic that is already minimal, since only a small number of managers query the dashboard periodically. Replicas are designed to scale read throughput or achieve very fast failover, neither of which is needed in this scenario.

questions.text: A healthcare company has deployed a patient records application backed by AlloyDB and set up IAM database authentication so that analysts can log in using their Google Cloud identities. The alloydb.databaseUser role has been granted to each analyst's IAM account, and the iam_auth database flag is enabled on the AlloyDB instance. Analysts confirm they can authenticate and open a session to the database without issue. However, when they attempt to SELECT from tables in the patient_data schema, they encounter "permission denied" errors. What is the appropriate way to fix this problem?

options.text: Enable the serviceusage.apiAdmin role on the analyst accounts
options.explanation:
The issue here is a gap between cloud-level authentication and database-level authorization, which must be resolved by issuing GRANT commands inside the PostgreSQL engine. When IAM database authentication is configured on AlloyDB, Google Cloud IAM handles verifying the user's identity and allowing them to connect to the instance. Once the connection is established, however, the database engine treats the authenticated user as a PostgreSQL role with no inherent privileges on any schema or table. The analysts need explicit GRANT statements to give them SELECT or other permissions on the patient_data schema and its tables. This is a fundamental aspect of how AlloyDB and PostgreSQL separate authentication from authorization.
Assigning the alloydb.admin role would give analysts broad control over the AlloyDB resource itself, such as managing clusters, creating backups, or modifying instance settings, but it would not grant any privileges inside the database engine. Enabling the serviceusage.apiAdmin role governs access to Google Cloud API configurations and has no relationship to SQL-level object permissions within a database. Applying the alloydb.client role at the project level controls whether a user can connect to AlloyDB instances through the Cloud API layer, but since the analysts can already authenticate and establish sessions, connectivity is clearly not the problem. None of these IAM-level roles translate into the ability to query specific tables or schemas, because the PostgreSQL security model operates independently from Google Cloud IAM once a session is active.

questions.text: A company runs a Cloud SQL for MySQL primary instance with Private IP in the us-east1 region. The infrastructure team has just provisioned a cross-region read replica in asia-southeast1, also configured with Private IP. Both instances connect to the organization's shared VPC through private services access. The replica consistently reports connection timeouts when attempting to replicate from the primary. The team has verified that no firewall rules are blocking traffic on the relevant ports. What must be changed to resolve the replication connectivity issue?

options.text: Configure a Cloud NAT gateway in both us-east1 and asia-southeast1 to translate internal traffic between the managed networks
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to enable custom route exchange on the VPC peering connection between your shared VPC and the Google-managed service producer network. Cloud SQL instances that use Private IP rely on VPC peering to connect to your network, and each instance in a different region sits behind a separate Google-managed VPC. VPC peering is non-transitive, meaning that traffic cannot automatically flow from one peered network through your VPC into another peered network. By enabling the export and import of custom routes on the peering configuration, your VPC can serve as a transit network, allowing the replica's managed network to learn the routes needed to reach the primary's managed network. Without this routing change, the network simply has no path between the two Cloud SQL endpoints, which explains the connection timeouts.
Deploying Cloud NAT gateways would not help because Cloud NAT handles outbound traffic to the internet and does not facilitate routing between privately peered networks. Creating a direct peering connection between the two service producer networks is not possible because those networks are owned and managed by Google, and customers cannot modify or peer them directly. Adding a static route in the shared VPC would not resolve the issue because the problem lies in the peering configuration itself, where routes are not being shared between the peered networks, and a manually added static route in your VPC cannot inject reachability into the Google-managed side of the peering.

questions.text: A financial technology company is developing a fraud detection engine that processes millions of transaction events per second from payment gateways across multiple regions. The system must deliver consistent sub-millisecond read latencies and remain fully operational even if an entire availability zone goes down. Traffic patterns are unpredictable, with sharp increases during flash sales and holiday shopping periods. You are responsible for designing the data storage layer on Google Cloud using Bigtable and ensuring the platform meets strict uptime requirements. What should you do?

options.text: Provision a Bigtable instance with clusters across multiple zones and establish performance monitoring
options.explanation:
The best approach is to provision a Bigtable instance that has clusters distributed across multiple zones along with performance monitoring in place. When a Bigtable instance is configured with multi-cluster routing and replication across zones, it achieves a higher availability SLA because traffic can automatically shift to a healthy cluster if one zone experiences an outage. Replication keeps data synchronized between the clusters, which provides both redundancy and the ability to spread read and write traffic more evenly. Adding performance monitoring ensures the team can observe whether latency stays within sub-millisecond targets and whether node capacity matches the workload, especially during unpredictable traffic surges.
Deploying multiple independent Bigtable instances and handling data partitioning in the application layer adds significant operational complexity without the built-in replication and automatic failover that a multi-cluster instance provides. Running a single cluster in a single zone creates a single point of failure, and tuning client-side write buffers only addresses localized throughput without solving the availability problem. Increasing the node count on a single instance can raise overall processing capacity, but it does nothing to protect against a zonal failure since all nodes would reside in the same zone. None of these alternatives address the structural redundancy needed to survive zone-level disruptions while maintaining consistent low-latency performance.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company operates a large on-premises Oracle database containing hundreds of stored procedures, custom triggers, and complex PL/SQL logic that supports its reporting platform. The engineering team has been tasked with migrating this workload to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL as part of a broader cloud-first initiative. Leadership wants the team to minimize manual development overhead during the code translation phase and keep all tooling within the Google Cloud platform. What is the recommended approach to handle the conversion of database-specific procedural code during this migration?

options.text: Export the schema and data directly, then troubleshoot incompatible syntax post-import
options.explanation:
The code conversion workspace in Database Migration Service is the best fit here because it provides a purpose-built environment within Google Cloud that automates the translation of dialect-specific procedural code such as stored procedures and triggers from one database engine to another. This workspace handles the heavy lifting of converting PL/SQL constructs into PL/pgSQL equivalents and flags specific areas where manual developer review is needed. It allows the team to manage schema transformation, review conversion results, and iterate on fixes all within a single integrated interface before performing the actual migration cutover. This dramatically reduces human error and accelerates the overall timeline compared to alternative approaches.
Manually translating every stored procedure and trigger is feasible but extremely labor-intensive, especially with hundreds of objects, and introduces a high risk of subtle bugs that are hard to detect in large codebases. Adding a middleware abstraction layer between the application and the database creates ongoing architectural complexity, adds latency to every query, and does not actually solve the underlying code compatibility problem. Exporting the schema and data directly into PostgreSQL without first converting Oracle-specific syntax will result in immediate import failures because PostgreSQL cannot interpret PL/SQL natively. Rehosting the Oracle database on a Compute Engine VM avoids the migration entirely and does not achieve the goal of moving to a fully managed Cloud SQL instance, which defeats the purpose of the cloud-first initiative.

questions.text: Your organization runs a microservices architecture where multiple independent services connect to a shared Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance. Lately, users have been reporting intermittent slowness, and your team suspects that one or two services are issuing expensive queries that degrade performance for everyone else. You need to determine which service is responsible and which specific queries are causing the problem. What should you do?

options.text: Use log messages produced by Cloud SQL
options.explanation:
Query Insights is the right tool here because it is purpose-built to help you analyze query-level performance in Cloud SQL. It allows you to break down database load by query, tag queries by application or service, and visualize how individual statements contribute to overall resource consumption over time. When multiple services share a single database instance, Query Insights lets you filter by application tags so you can isolate exactly which service is generating the problematic queries. This makes it straightforward to correlate periods of degraded performance with specific workloads from specific services.
Cloud SQL instance monitoring in the Google Cloud Console gives you a general health overview of the database, such as connection counts and storage usage, but it does not let you drill into individual queries or attribute them to particular services. The Cloud Monitoring dashboard with Cloud SQL metrics focuses on infrastructure-level signals like CPU utilization, memory pressure, and disk throughput, which can tell you something is wrong but cannot pinpoint the exact queries or services responsible. Log messages from Cloud SQL can capture slow query entries, but parsing raw logs to identify patterns across multiple services is labor-intensive and does not provide the interactive visualization or application-level tagging needed to efficiently troubleshoot in a multi-service environment.

questions.text: A fintech company runs a multi-tier application on Google Kubernetes Engine. The backend services need to communicate with an AlloyDB instance that has been provisioned with Private Service Access and has no public IP address. The security team requires that all database connections are authenticated using IAM-based identity verification and encrypted in transit without manually managing SSL certificates. You want to minimize infrastructure overhead and operational costs. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy a dedicated Compute Engine instance running a SOCKS proxy to relay traffic between GKE pods and the AlloyDB instance
options.explanation:
The AlloyDB Auth Proxy running as a sidecar in GKE is the best approach here because it natively handles both IAM-based authentication and TLS encryption on behalf of the application. By deploying the proxy alongside the application containers, the connection to AlloyDB is automatically secured without requiring developers to manage certificate lifecycles or configure complex networking. This method integrates naturally with Kubernetes service accounts and Google Cloud IAM, providing a streamlined and cost-effective way to enforce identity-based access control while leveraging the existing private network path to the database.
Setting up a Cloud VPN tunnel between the GKE cluster and the AlloyDB subnet is unnecessary because both resources already communicate over the internal Google Cloud network through Private Service Access, making a VPN an added cost with no security benefit. Connecting directly to the private IP address is technically possible but does not satisfy the requirement of IAM-based authentication and automatic encryption without manual certificate management. Running a SOCKS proxy on a separate Compute Engine instance introduces an additional virtual machine that must be maintained and paid for, creates a potential single point of failure, and offers no advantage over the native Auth Proxy tooling that already integrates with Kubernetes workloads.

questions.text: A large healthcare analytics firm needs to migrate its patient billing database from an on-premises MySQL deployment to Cloud SQL using Database Migration Service. The billing system processes thousands of transactions per minute during operating hours and any increase in query latency could delay claim submissions. The database team wants to complete the initial data load as fast as possible without degrading the responsiveness of the production billing application. What approach should the team take?

options.text: Launch multiple separate migration jobs that each target the same Cloud SQL destination instance
options.explanation:
The best approach is to run the migration against a dedicated read replica rather than the primary instance while taking advantage of maximum parallelism to accelerate the initial data load. A read replica absorbs the heavy I/O and CPU demands of the bulk data extraction, which means the primary database remains fully available to serve the billing application without any added latency. Setting initial load parallelism to its highest value allows Database Migration Service to use multiple threads simultaneously, moving data much faster than a single-threaded approach. Once the initial load completes, the migration job transitions to reading binary logs to capture ongoing changes and keep the Cloud SQL target synchronized with the source.
Launching multiple independent migration jobs against the same destination can lead to data consistency problems and create contention on the Cloud SQL instance as concurrent jobs attempt to write overlapping datasets. Upgrading the hardware on the primary server is a costly workaround that does not eliminate the resource contention between migration workloads and production queries running on the same instance. Directing a single high-parallelism migration job at the primary instance still forces the production billing system to compete with the migration process for CPU, memory, and disk throughput, which risks the exact latency spikes the team is trying to avoid.

questions.text: A multinational insurance company runs its policy management database on a Cloud SQL for MySQL Enterprise Plus instance using version 8.0.31 in the us-central1 region. Due to new industry regulations requiring documented disaster recovery capabilities with minimal data loss, the team needs to establish a DR solution in the us-east4 region that can be routinely validated without impacting production workloads. What is the most appropriate approach to meet these requirements?

options.text: Deploy a cross-region read replica on version 8.0.31 in us-east4 and use the gcloud switchover command to validate the DR plan
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to create a cross-region read replica running the identical database version in the target region and then use the managed switchover command to perform non-disruptive DR testing. Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus supports an advanced disaster recovery workflow where a designated cross-region replica can participate in a coordinated role swap with the primary instance. The switchover command orchestrates this gracefully by pausing writes on the primary, waiting for the replica to fully catch up to the most recent transaction, and then reversing the roles of both instances. This ensures near-zero data loss and allows repeated testing without permanently altering the topology, which directly satisfies the regulatory requirement to routinely validate disaster recovery readiness.
A cascading replica does not have a direct replication relationship with the primary instance, which means the automated switchover workflow cannot be used with it. The switchover mechanism specifically requires a direct primary-to-replica link to coordinate synchronization and role reversal. Using a replica on a different minor version such as 8.0.32 would cause the switchover to fail because the orchestration process enforces strict version parity between the primary and the DR replica to ensure compatibility during the role swap. The promote-replica command converts a replica into a standalone primary instance, but this is a one-way operation that permanently breaks the replication link and does not include the synchronization checks that the switchover command provides. Promotion is intended as a reactive measure during an actual outage when the primary is unreachable, not as a mechanism to validate DR plans on a recurring basis without disrupting the production environment.

questions.text: A mid-size e-commerce company runs its backend on AlloyDB for PostgreSQL. During a flash sale event, the operations team notices significantly increased query latency across their cluster. They suspect that certain microservices or API endpoints are generating disproportionately heavy database traffic, but they need to pinpoint exactly which ones. Which tool or feature should they leverage to trace the relationship between specific application routes and the resulting database performance impact?

options.text: Cloud Logging filters
options.explanation:
### Explanation
AlloyDB Query Insights is the right choice because it offers a built-in dashboard that surfaces top queries alongside application-level metadata such as tags and request routes. This enables the operations team to directly see which microservices or API endpoints are driving the most database load during the flash sale. The feature captures wait events, execution counts, and latency breakdowns, all tied back to the application context that generated the queries. It aggregates this data historically, which means the team can examine trends over the duration of the event rather than only seeing a snapshot. This built-in correlation between application-level attributes and database performance makes root cause analysis straightforward without requiring custom tooling or manual joins across data sources.
Cloud Logging filters can surface log entries based on timestamps or severity, but they lack the granular query-level performance metrics needed to isolate which application component is causing database bottlenecks. The Index Advisor is designed to recommend index changes that could speed up specific queries, but it does not help identify which part of the application stack is responsible for generating heavy load. questions.text: A logistics company operates a fleet of thousands of delivery vehicles, each transmitting GPS coordinates, speed, engine diagnostics, and fuel levels every few seconds. The engineering team needs a database that can handle millions of writes per second and allow analysts to query recent location history and diagnostics by vehicle ID across specific time windows. The data is growing at several terabytes per day, and the team wants the system to scale out seamlessly as the fleet expands without having to manage partition strategies manually. Which Google Cloud database is the best fit?

options.text: Firestore
options.explanation:
Cloud Bigtable is the right choice here because it is purpose-built to handle massive volumes of sequential writes and efficient range-based lookups, which is exactly what this vehicle telemetry scenario demands. With millions of writes per second and terabytes of daily data growth, the system needs a database that thrives under sustained high-throughput ingestion. Bigtable organizes data by row key, making it natural to design keys around vehicle ID and timestamp so that queries retrieving a specific vehicle's history over a time window translate into fast, sequential range scans. Scaling is handled by simply adding nodes, and the system automatically redistributes data across them without requiring manual sharding or partition management.
Cloud SQL is a managed relational database that primarily scales vertically, which makes it unsuitable when write volumes reach millions of operations per second across terabytes of daily data. Cloud Spanner provides horizontal scaling and strong consistency but is optimized for transactional relational workloads with complex queries and cross-region consistency requirements, not sustained append-heavy telemetry ingestion at this scale. Firestore is a document-oriented database designed around application data with flexible schemas and real-time synchronization, and it would struggle with the write throughput and sequential access patterns described in this scenario.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm operates an on-premises PostgreSQL database that stores patient billing and insurance claim records. The company plans to move this database to Cloud SQL on Google Cloud. Due to regulatory requirements, the database must never be reachable from the public internet, and all user access must be tied to verifiable identities rather than shared credentials. Which two steps should the architect take to satisfy these requirements?

options.text: Create a Private Service Connect endpoint to reach the Cloud SQL instance
options.explanation:
The two steps that directly address the stated requirements are creating a Private Service Connect endpoint and enabling IAM database authentication on an internal-only Cloud SQL instance. A Private Service Connect endpoint allows traffic from the corporate network or connected VPCs to reach the Cloud SQL instance entirely over Google's private network, ensuring that the database is never exposed to the public internet. This satisfies the regulatory constraint that patient billing data must remain inaccessible from external networks. IAM database authentication replaces traditional username and password credentials with short-lived tokens linked to specific Google Cloud identities, which fulfills the requirement that all access be tied to verifiable identities rather than shared static passwords. Restricting the instance to an internal IP address removes any possibility of public routing and adds another layer of network isolation.
Configuring an external IP address, even when paired with Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, still exposes the instance to the public internet and increases the attack surface, which directly conflicts with the requirement that the database never be publicly reachable. Automated backups and point-in-time recovery are important operational practices that protect against data loss, but they do not address network privacy or identity-based access control. Transfer Appliance with Customer-Managed Encryption Keys is a method of physically shipping large datasets to Google Cloud, which can be useful during an initial migration but does not govern how the Cloud SQL instance is accessed or secured on an ongoing basis. Firewall rules layered on top of a public endpoint are never as secure as eliminating the public endpoint entirely.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database in the europe-west1 region that powers their real-time trading platform. The engineering team has been tasked with building a disaster recovery plan that ensures database operations can resume in a separate region if europe-west1 experiences a complete outage. The recovery strategy must minimize data loss and allow the application to begin accepting write traffic again as quickly as possible. Which approach should the team implement?

options.text: Set up automated database exports on a recurring schedule to a multi-regional Cloud Storage bucket
options.explanation:
Promoting a cross-region read replica is the most effective strategy here because it keeps an asynchronously replicated copy of the database in a geographically separate region. If the primary region suffers a complete outage, the team can promote the read replica in the secondary region to become a fully independent primary instance capable of handling both read and write operations. This promotion process requires manual or automated intervention but provides the fastest path to restoring service with minimal data loss, since the replica continuously receives updates from the primary. This is a well-established disaster recovery pattern used by organizations that need a warm standby in a different geographic location.
Configuring high availability within the same region only protects against a single zone going down and would not help if the entire region became unavailable. Scheduling automated exports to Cloud Storage creates periodic snapshots of the data, but restoring from an export involves provisioning a new instance and importing the data, which takes significantly longer and results in more data loss compared to a replica that stays nearly current. Running an external replica on a Compute Engine instance introduces operational complexity and lacks the managed failover capabilities that Cloud SQL provides natively, making it a less reliable and harder-to-maintain option during an actual regional disaster.

questions.text: A gaming company is building a real-time leaderboard and player state service on Google Cloud. The system must deliver sub-millisecond response times when reading and writing player data stored as key-value pairs. The total dataset is expected to grow beyond 600 GB as the player base expands globally. The engineering team also requires that the database be accessible from their application VPC through a service-oriented endpoint model that does not rely on VPC peering with the managed service network. Which architecture should the team implement?

options.text: Memorystore Redis Cluster with Private Service Connect
options.explanation:
The correct choice here is the Redis Cluster option paired with the service-oriented connectivity model that avoids VPC peering. A Redis Cluster topology supports horizontal scaling across multiple shards, which allows the system to handle a dataset well beyond 600 GB while still maintaining the sub-millisecond latency that Redis is known for. Private Service Connect creates an endpoint within the application VPC that routes traffic to the managed service without requiring a peering relationship between the two networks. This gives the engineering team a cleaner network boundary and avoids common issues like overlapping IP ranges that come with traditional peering setups.
A standard Redis Instance with Private Service Access would use VPC peering under the hood, which directly conflicts with the requirement to avoid peering with the service provider network. A standard Redis Instance paired with Private Service Connect would satisfy the networking constraint but would not be able to scale to 600 GB because standalone instances have significantly lower memory limits compared to a cluster deployment. Cloud Bigtable can handle large datasets and offers low latency, but it is not a Redis-compatible in-memory store and would not natively support the key-value caching patterns typically used in leaderboard and session state scenarios without substantial application redesign.

questions.text: A multinational e-commerce platform has recently migrated its product catalog and order analytics database to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL. During seasonal sales events like Black Friday and holiday promotions, the platform sees enormous increases in read-heavy reporting queries that power merchant dashboards and inventory insights. You must architect a solution that accommodates these demand fluctuations while keeping infrastructure costs as low as possible. What approach should you take?

options.text: Route all reporting and analytical queries to the primary read-write instance to reduce architectural complexity
options.explanation:
The right approach here is to dynamically adjust the node count in the AlloyDB read pool according to observed workload patterns and metrics. AlloyDB separates compute from storage, meaning the read pool can be scaled horizontally without impacting the primary instance or requiring data duplication at the storage level. During seasonal sales events, adding nodes to the read pool increases concurrency and throughput so that merchant dashboards remain responsive. When demand subsides after the event, removing those nodes eliminates unnecessary compute costs. This elasticity is a core advantage of the cloud-native architecture that AlloyDB provides, and it directly ties infrastructure spending to actual usage.
Sending all analytical queries to the primary instance creates resource contention between read and write workloads, which risks degrading transactional performance during the most critical business periods. Deploying a fixed, oversized read pool ensures availability but results in paying continuously for capacity that sits idle during the majority of the year when traffic is normal. This approach ignores the cost-efficiency requirement entirely. Upgrading the machine type of individual nodes is a vertical scaling strategy that lacks the flexibility and granularity of horizontal scaling through node count adjustments. Vertical scaling also typically requires restarts or downtime and cannot respond as smoothly to the rapid demand changes that characterize flash sales or promotional events.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company operates a Cloud SQL for MySQL instance in the europe-west1 region that powers a real-time patient monitoring dashboard used by hospitals around the clock. The database team has been asked to guarantee a 99.99% availability SLA and ensure that routine infrastructure maintenance events cause no more than a few seconds of disruption. The solution must work transparently with the existing application without requiring changes to connection handling or retry logic. What should you do?

options.text: Create a cross-region read replica in europe-west3
options.explanation:
The best approach is to upgrade the Cloud SQL instance to the Enterprise Plus edition. This edition is specifically built to support mission-critical workloads that demand a 99.99% availability SLA. A key technical benefit of the Enterprise Plus tier is its ability to complete maintenance operations with near-zero downtime, typically under 10 seconds. This is accomplished through optimized failover mechanisms and tighter integration with the underlying infrastructure that the standard Enterprise edition does not offer. Because the switchover is so fast and handled transparently, there is no need to modify application connection logic or add custom retry handling.
Setting up a cross-region read replica in another region is useful when you need geographic redundancy or want to serve read traffic closer to users in a different location, but it does not change how the primary instance behaves during scheduled maintenance. Configuring a preferred maintenance window simply lets you choose when the update occurs, which can help align downtime with quieter periods, but it does nothing to reduce the actual duration of the interruption itself. Enabling automated backups with binary logging and extended retention is a data protection measure that helps you recover from accidental deletions or corruption, but it has no effect on instance availability during planned infrastructure updates.

questions.text: A large healthcare analytics firm needs to replicate its on-premises PostgreSQL patient scheduling databases into BigQuery so that operational teams can monitor appointment volumes and staffing needs through near real-time dashboards. The team wants a fully managed approach that avoids building custom extraction logic and delivers data to BigQuery with minimal delay. Which solution best meets these requirements?

options.text: Use Database Migration Service to migrate data into a Cloud SQL instance and then access it through BigQuery external datasets
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use Datastream combined with Dataflow to create a managed, low-latency replication pipeline from the on-premises PostgreSQL databases into BigQuery. Datastream is purpose-built to perform change data capture (CDC) from relational databases like PostgreSQL, streaming inserts, updates, and deletes into Cloud Storage with minimal lag. Dataflow then reads those change events and applies them directly to BigQuery tables. This architecture handles scaling, connectivity, and schema evolution automatically, giving the healthcare firm the near real-time data availability it needs without requiring any custom extraction code.
Using Cloud Data Fusion with a JDBC connector introduces a batch or micro-batch pattern that adds more latency than a dedicated CDC stream and requires more configuration overhead to approach real-time speeds. Migrating data through Database Migration Service into Cloud SQL and then querying it via BigQuery external datasets adds an unnecessary intermediate database layer, and federated queries against Cloud SQL typically perform worse than querying data stored natively in BigQuery. Building a custom application that exports CSV files and runs periodic BigQuery Load jobs is inherently batch-oriented and fragile, making it unable to meet the near real-time requirement while also increasing operational burden on the team.

questions.text: A financial services company runs a high-traffic online trading platform that relies on Memorystore for Redis to cache frequently accessed market data and user portfolio summaries. The company is expanding its disaster recovery strategy to handle a complete regional outage. During a failover event, the engineering team must be able to bring up a new Redis instance in a secondary region and populate it with the most recent cache data from the primary region. What approach should the team implement to meet this requirement?

options.text: Configure RDB snapshots at a high frequency on the instance
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to export Redis data into a Cloud Storage bucket configured with dual-region or multi-region storage. By storing the exported RDB file in a dual-region bucket, the data remains accessible even when the primary region is completely unavailable. When the failover event occurs, the team can spin up a new Memorystore instance in the secondary region and import the most recent export file, ensuring the cache is hydrated with real data rather than starting cold. This process can be automated on a schedule to keep the exported data as fresh as possible, minimizing the gap between the last export and the point of failure.
Enabling High Availability on a Redis cluster provides resilience across zones within a single region, but it does not replicate data to a geographically separate region, so it would not survive a total regional outage. Configuring RDB snapshots at a high frequency captures point-in-time backups of the instance, but these snapshots are managed internally by the service and are tied to the region where the instance exists, making them inaccessible if that entire region goes down. Deploying a Standard Tier instance with multiple read replicas improves read throughput and provides zonal redundancy, but all replicas reside within the same region as the primary node, which means a full regional failure would take out both the primary and all of its replicas.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm stores patient encounter records in Cloud Spanner as their system of record. Their data science team needs to periodically run resource-intensive aggregate queries and join large tables in BigQuery to build predictive models. The operations team is concerned that these analytical workloads will degrade the latency of real-time clinical applications that rely on the same Spanner database. How should you address this concern while enabling the data science team to perform their work?

options.text: Add a secondary index on the columns frequently referenced in the analytical queries
options.explanation:
Spanner Data Boost is the right solution here because it provides an independent set of compute resources that handle heavy read operations without touching the CPU or memory allocated to the production Spanner nodes. When combined with BigQuery federated queries, the data science team can run complex aggregations and joins directly against Spanner data while the analytical processing is completely offloaded from the primary instance. This means the real-time clinical applications continue to operate at normal latency levels with no degradation. Data Boost is serverless, so there is no need to provision or manage extra infrastructure, and costs are incurred only when the analytical queries are actually running.
Adding a secondary index may improve query performance on specific columns, but it does not isolate the read workload from the production instance, so the clinical applications would still experience resource contention during heavy analytical queries. Scaling up the node count before each job provides additional capacity, but the analytical and transactional workloads would still share the same compute environment, and this approach introduces operational overhead and unnecessary costs during periods when no analytical work is happening. Running a Dataflow pipeline with many workers can parallelize the data movement, but the source reads still hit the production Spanner nodes directly, placing significant load on the same resources that serve clinical traffic. None of these alternatives achieve the true compute isolation that is required to protect the production environment from analytical workload interference.

After onboarding several new institutional clients, the database storage consumption has steadily climbed to 80% of the maximum allowed capacity. The engineering team is concerned that continued growth will cause write failures during the next trading window. You need to take immediate action to raise the storage ceiling so the instance can accommodate the growing dataset without service disruption. What should you do?

options.text: Set up an automated archival pipeline to transfer older records to Cloud Storage
options.explanation:
Cloud Spanner ties its maximum storage allowance directly to the compute capacity provisioned on the instance. Adding processing units or nodes immediately raises the storage limit, allowing the database to continue accepting writes without hitting its ceiling. This operation can be performed online with no downtime, making it the correct and most immediate response when storage utilization is approaching the maximum threshold. It is the standard approach recommended by Google Cloud when a Spanner instance needs more room to grow.
Moving to a multi-regional configuration changes how data is replicated across regions and improves availability and global read latency, but it does not alter the fundamental relationship between compute and storage on the instance. Archiving historical records to Cloud Storage could eventually reduce storage usage, but designing and executing a data migration during a period of active growth introduces risk and latency that makes it unsuitable as an immediate fix. Deploying a read-only replica in another zone enhances fault tolerance and can distribute read traffic, but replicas do not expand the storage quota available to the primary instance. The only direct mechanism to raise the storage limit in Cloud Spanner is to increase the allocated compute resources.

questions.text: A logistics company runs a real-time tracking platform that stores shipment and driver location data in Cloud Bigtable. After acquiring a regional competitor and integrating their fleet into the platform, the team noticed that read and write latencies have become inconsistent, with some requests taking significantly longer than others. Certain tablets appear to be handling a disproportionate share of traffic. The engineering team wants to resolve the performance degradation while keeping the architecture straightforward and manageable. What should you do? (Select two)

options.text: Switch all application traffic to multi-cluster routing across every available region
options.explanation:
The performance issues stem from hotspotting and uneven request routing, both of which commonly surface when a new and different workload is introduced into an existing Bigtable instance. Redesigning the row key schema directly addresses the uneven tablet load by ensuring that writes and reads spread more evenly across nodes, which prevents a small number of tablet servers from becoming bottlenecks. When access patterns change due to onboarding a new dataset, the original key design may no longer distribute traffic well, making schema adjustments essential. Configuring app profiles with single-cluster routing gives the team precise control over which cluster serves the new workload, reducing latency variability that comes from unpredictable routing decisions. Together, these two changes target both the data layer and the request routing layer without adding significant operational overhead.
Archiving old shipment data to Cloud Storage would reduce storage volume but would not fix the access pattern issues causing hotspots on active data. Reducing the number of nodes in the cluster removes available capacity, which would likely worsen latency and contention rather than resolve it. Enabling multi-cluster routing across all regions could cause requests to be served by geographically distant clusters, which introduces additional latency and inconsistency rather than improving the situation. None of these alternatives address the root causes of uneven data distribution or unpredictable request routing.

questions.text: A healthcare technology company maintains patient appointment and billing records in a Cloud Spanner database. The company's finance team occasionally needs to run complex aggregation queries to analyze revenue trends and billing patterns. These queries happen only a few times per month. The company wants to keep costs as low as possible while ensuring that only authorized finance team members can access the data. What solution should you recommend?

options.text: Set up a Spanner change stream to continuously replicate data modifications into a BigQuery table and restrict access using IAM
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to use BigQuery federated queries against the Spanner database. Because the finance team only runs these analytical queries a few times per month, there is no need to maintain a separate copy of the data or run ongoing data pipelines. Federated queries let BigQuery read directly from Spanner at query time, meaning the company only pays for compute when a query actually executes. IAM permissions on the BigQuery connection object and dataset ensure that only authorized finance team members can run these queries, satisfying the security requirement without additional infrastructure.
Scheduling a Dataflow batch pipeline to export data into BigLake introduces unnecessary costs from both the Dataflow job execution and the additional storage of exported data, which is wasteful when queries only run a few times per month. Setting up a Spanner change stream with continuous replication into BigQuery is designed for near-real-time analytics and dashboarding workloads, and the always-on processing pipeline would generate ongoing costs that far exceed what is justified by occasional queries. Creating a view directly in Spanner would force complex analytical aggregations to run on the transactional database engine, which lacks the specialized columnar processing capabilities of BigQuery and could require scaling up the Spanner instance to handle the analytical workload without impacting transactional performance. That tradeoff would likely increase costs rather than minimize them.

questions.text: Your healthcare analytics company manages a patient activity tracking system that currently runs on Amazon DynamoDB. The engineering team has determined that Cloud Bigtable is better suited to handle the growing time-series workloads and upcoming machine learning initiatives. You need to migrate the data with minimal downtime while ensuring that records remain consistent between the two systems throughout the transition period. The solution should minimize operational overhead and avoid unnecessary infrastructure management. What should you do?

options.text: Use a Dataproc cluster to extract records from DynamoDB and batch load them into Bigtable
options.explanation:
The best approach is to create a managed Dataflow pipeline that leverages the DynamoDBIO and BigtableIO connectors to continuously synchronize data between the two systems. Dataflow is a fully managed service that eliminates the need to provision or maintain infrastructure, which directly addresses the requirement to minimize operational overhead. Because it supports streaming, new and updated records in DynamoDB can be propagated to Bigtable with very low latency, keeping downtime to a minimum. The purpose-built connectors reduce the amount of custom code required and simplify consistency handling between the source and target databases. This approach also scales automatically as data volumes increase, which aligns well with the growing workload demands described in the scenario.
Spinning up a Dataproc cluster introduces cluster provisioning and management responsibilities that add operational burden and is better suited to batch processing rather than continuous synchronization. Writing a custom application requires significant development effort, ongoing maintenance, and carries a higher risk of bugs or partial failures during the replication process. Exporting to CSV and importing with the cbt tool is a batch-oriented workflow that does not maintain ongoing consistency between the two databases and would likely require downtime or repeated manual export cycles. None of these alternatives offer the combination of near real-time replication, minimal operational effort, and built-in fault tolerance that a managed Dataflow pipeline provides.

questions.text: A logistics company has been running a continuous migration from their self-managed PostgreSQL instance to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL using the Database Migration Service. The migration job has been active and replicating changes successfully for several days. A developer has just added a new inventory tracking table to the source database as part of a sprint release, and the team needs this table to appear in the destination Cloud SQL instance as part of the same replication stream. What is the correct way to include this newly created table in the ongoing migration?

options.text: Navigate to the Database Migration Service job settings in the Cloud Console and edit the table filter to include the new table
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use the pglogical extension directly on the source database to add the new table to the replication set that the Database Migration Service relies on. When continuous replication is powered by pglogical, the extension maintains replication sets that define which tables are streamed to the destination. New tables created after the migration job has started are not automatically enrolled in replication. By calling pglogical.replication_set_add_table, you explicitly register the table with the active replication set, and the logical decoding process begins capturing changes from that table without requiring any disruption to the running migration job.
Stopping the migration job and starting over would mean re-dumping the entire database, which wastes significant time and compute resources just to pick up a single new table. The Database Migration Service interface in the Cloud Console does not offer a way to modify the table inclusion list while a job is already running, so attempting to edit the job configuration would not work. Exporting and importing the table independently through pg_dump creates a snapshot that is not tied to the ongoing replication stream, which introduces the risk of data inconsistency since future changes to that table would still not be captured by the migration job. questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is deploying a new patient data aggregation service built on Cloud Run. The compliance team requires that the managed Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance storing patient records must have zero public internet exposure and must communicate exclusively over internal Google Cloud networking. The database has been set up with internal-only routing to meet this requirement. What combination of networking and connection management components should the engineering team configure so the Cloud Run service can reliably communicate with the database?

options.text: Provision the Cloud SQL instance with a private IP, enable Private Service Access on the VPC, and configure a Serverless VPC Access connector while using the Cloud SQL Auth proxy
options.explanation:
The correct approach combines a private IP, Private Service Access, a Serverless VPC Access connector, and a connection pool. Giving the Cloud SQL instance a private IP through Private Service Access ensures the database exists within the VPC address space and all traffic stays on Google's internal network with no public endpoint. Cloud Run services do not natively operate inside a customer VPC, so a Serverless VPC Access connector is needed to route traffic from Cloud Run into the VPC where the database lives. A connection pool is the right connection management choice here because Cloud Run can scale instances rapidly, and each new instance may attempt to open its own database connections. Pooling consolidates and reuses those connections, preventing the database from being overwhelmed during traffic spikes.
Options that assign a public IP to the Cloud SQL instance fail the compliance requirement regardless of any firewall rules applied. Even if firewall rules restrict which services can connect, the database still has a public endpoint, which violates the mandate of zero internet exposure. Adding a connection pool or auth proxy in that scenario improves connection handling or security, but neither eliminates the fundamental problem of a publicly addressable database. The option that uses a private IP with Private Service Access and a Serverless VPC Access connector but substitutes the Cloud SQL Auth proxy instead of a connection pool addresses the networking isolation correctly. However, the Cloud SQL Auth proxy handles authentication and encryption rather than managing the volume of concurrent connections. In a serverless environment where instances can scale unpredictably, connection pooling is the more critical component to prevent connection exhaustion at the database layer.

questions.text: A ridesharing platform stores real-time GPS pings from 200,000 active drivers in a Bigtable cluster, processing up to 3 million writes per second during evening rush hours. The row key follows the pattern driver_id#epoch_millis, and monitoring reveals that certain tablet servers are consistently overwhelmed while others sit nearly idle. The company retains all location history indefinitely, resulting in a 750TB dataset that is straining the infrastructure budget. Only the most recent 90 days of data is actively queried by the trip-matching engine, which requires read latency under 8ms to maintain service-level objectives. What combination of changes should the team implement?

options.text: Partition the data into separate tables by geographic region, deploy multi-region Bigtable instances, and export cold records into BigQuery
options.explanation:
The best approach here combines three targeted fixes that each address a distinct problem the team is facing. Adding a hash-based prefix to the driver_id portion of the row key eliminates the hotspotting issue by ensuring that writes are distributed evenly across all nodes in the Bigtable cluster, rather than concentrating traffic on a few tablet servers that happen to own adjacent key ranges. Enabling autoscaling allows the cluster to expand during evening rush periods when write volume peaks at 3 million operations per second and contract during quieter hours, which keeps read latency well below the 8ms threshold while controlling costs. Archiving data older than 90 days to Cloud Storage dramatically reduces the 750TB storage footprint in Bigtable, since the vast majority of that data is never touched by the trip-matching engine and Cloud Storage offers a far more cost-effective tier.
Simply scaling the cluster to more nodes does not fix the root cause of hotspotting because the same skewed key distribution will continue to overload certain tablets regardless of cluster size, and GRS replication adds cost and complexity without addressing either the key distribution or the storage budget problem. Leading the row key with a timestamp is a well-documented Bigtable anti-pattern that would funnel all concurrent writes to a single tablet server, making the hotspotting dramatically worse, and keeping the entire 750TB dataset on SSD storage would increase costs rather than reduce them. Splitting data into separate tables by geographic region introduces operational overhead and does not inherently solve uneven key distribution within each table, while exporting cold data to BigQuery is more expensive than Cloud Storage and is not the appropriate destination when the goal is simply low-cost archival rather than analytical querying.
